access.accessMethod: Public bibliographic records
access.entityLabel: Fisher, Richardson, and Fisher, An Anthology of Chancery English (1984)

bibliographic.citation: John H. Fisher, Malcolm Richardson, and Jane L. Fisher, eds., An Anthology of Chancery English (Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1984), xvii, 416 pp., 6 plates
bibliographic.title: An Anthology of Chancery English

dateStatements.label: publication

entities.description: A scholarly anthology containing 241 transcribed documentary texts plus introduction, apparatus, bibliography, glossary/index material, and plates. ChancEng encodes only the selected texts on pp. 84–303.
entities.label: Fisher, Richardson, and Fisher, An Anthology of Chancery English (1984)
notes: Benskin's critique and his report of Matheson's findings show that the transcription and apparatus must not be treated as diplomatic or error-free without witness checking.

physicalDescription.extent: xvii, 416 pages and 6 pages of plates
physicalDescription.format: Scholarly monograph and documentary text edition
notes: Includes bibliographical references on pp. 52–62 and an index.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1984. Medieval source documents may be public domain, but post-1977 editorial authorship is not made public domain by source age or U-M's separate digital-item label.
rights.component: Original selection, transcriptions, editorial apparatus, and presentation of the 1984 edition
notes: The current rightsholder and any publication notice were not established from the controlled scan; seek permission for substantial republication or commercial derivative use.

access.accessMethod: Direct official journal PDF
access.entityLabel: Pérez Quintero's scholarly description of the Chancery corpus

localCopies.label: Complete official Pérez Quintero article PDF
notes: The 25-page provider PDF uses RC4 encryption; its permission flags allow printing but restrict copying/extraction and modification. qpdf checks the file successfully while reporting shared-object and hint-table warnings; Poppler read all 25 pages. The downloaded bytes are preserved without repair.

localCopies.label: Complete Pérez Quintero article PDF, Dialnet mirror
notes: This Dialnet copy is textually near-identical but byte-distinct from the official journal PDF. It also uses RC4 encryption; qpdf checks it successfully with shared-object and hint-table warnings, and Poppler read all 25 pages. The downloaded bytes are preserved without repair.

entities.description: Archival source cited by the anthology for item 174 as SC8/24/1197. The current Discovery audit resolved it exactly as SC 8/24/1197 (C9061368). Current catalog description: AP Commons of parliament. Lords of parliament. The commons complain that many people arrested for treason, felony, Lollardy and such crimes, are sent at the King's command to the Tower of London and other castles, where they remain a long time without any process against them; which is very expensive, dangerous to their keepers, and likely to encourage other malefactors. They ask the lord to ordain that all such prisoners in future might appear quickly before temporal or spiritual judges, as the case requires, and either be released or be punished. [On face] Soit baille a lez seignurs (It is to be delivered to the lords).[On dorse: none] The Tower of London, [London].

notes: Anthology label: Cambridgeshire Indenture.

The electronic XML/U-M heading says ‘Cambridgeshire Indenture’; Benskin reports that the printed anthology calls this item ‘Lincoln indenture’. The current catalog describes an agreement concerning a chapter-house at Stamford, and the document is dated at Stamford, in Lincolnshire.
The exact print-versus-electronic heading divergence remains to be checked against a controlled print scan.

next_steps: Collate each text against a facsimile or repository reproduction.
Record supplied text, expansions, deletions, insertions, lineation, punctuation, and omissions item by item.
Prioritize items identified by Benskin and Matheson as erroneous.
openQuestions.question: Which of the anthology's 241 transcriptions and editorial interventions are accurate when collated against the physical records or new facsimiles?

next_steps: Obtain written clarification from U-M/OTA and the 1984 edition rightsholder before commercial reuse of the electronic text or substantial edition-derived material.
Request separate publication permission for TNA or BL images where provider terms require it.
Treat local caches as research evidence, not redistributable assets, unless an item-specific license permits publication.
openQuestions.question: Can ChancEng-derived text, formatting research, or facsimile images be redistributed through a repository or commercial/POD workflow?

next_steps: Use printed apparatus and series-specific finding aids to interpret legacy references.
Ask TNA staff to resolve colon-separated, malformed, joined-fragment, and superseded references.
Update each witness entity without deleting the inherited literal reference.
openQuestions.question: Can the 145 TNA references currently resolved only to parent or ancestor records be reconciled to exact modern item-level records?

next_steps: Request reference-image quotes from BL digitisation services, preferably for complete codices when feasible.
Ask BL whether the historical full digital coverage for Vespasian F XIII/1 can be restored or supplied.
Record exact folio/canvas mappings and provider reuse terms for any supplied images.
openQuestions.question: Can complete or relevant-leaf facsimiles be obtained for Cotton MSS Galba B I, Julius B VI, Vespasian F I, Vespasian F XIII/1, and Cleopatra F IV?

next_steps: Inspect the controlled print scan at item 238 and record the exact printed heading.
Determine whether the electronic heading reflects an encoding error, a pre-publication source, or a later editorial change.
Preserve the document's Stamford/Lincolnshire evidence separately from either editorial heading.
openQuestions.question: Why does the XML/U-M version label item 238 ‘Cambridgeshire Indenture’ while Benskin reports the printed anthology heading as ‘Lincoln indenture’ for the Stamford agreement?

next_steps: Ask BL to reconcile the catalog description, physical foliation, and IIIF canvas label.
Continue to cite canvas 325 as the verified digital location while preserving both folio reports.
openQuestions.question: Which foliation should be cited for anthology item 14 in Cotton MS Vespasian C XII: the edition/provider's f. 155r or the current catalog's f. 154r?

next_steps: Report the self-link/display-number defect to OTA.
Retain the version relation based on the reciprocal 1284 record and deposit chronology.
openQuestions.question: Can OTA correct record 1676's internal note, which labels/links the earlier version inconsistently?

next_steps: Compare milestone order against the 1984 print scan under controlled access.
Do not infer missing edition pages or manuscript content from milestone anomalies alone.
notes: Absent PB labels: 141, 192, 193, and 293; several labels occur out of numeric order, including 208/215/216 and 270/287/271.
openQuestions.question: Are the missing and out-of-order XML page milestones inherited conversion defects, and do they affect generated page-reference navigation?

relations.subjectLabel: Fisher, Richardson, and Fisher, An Anthology of Chancery English (1984)

reviewNotes: The 241 edition-to-witness relations record source claims, not blanket verification of transcription fidelity; only BL items 14 and 70 were visually matched directly in this review.
TNA audit coverage is complete for all 231 TNA-cited items: 86 exact current-reference matches, 145 parent/ancestor matches, 31 records flagged digitized, and 200 without a public digitized copy.

The 231 TNA mappings resolve to 120 unique current catalog records because many legacy references reach shared parent pieces; repeated current IDs must not be mistaken for proof that the selected source documents are identical.

Complete facsimile bundles mean every canvas exposed by the reviewed provider manifest was cached, including binding and blanks where exposed; they do not claim preservation-master resolution or broaden provider reuse terms.

The Benskin cache is complete for the publisher preview but partial for the full chapter; both the official Pérez Quintero article PDF and a byte-distinct Dialnet mirror are cached completely and preserved without PDF repair.

Known high-value corrections include item 1's date/office, items 106, 107, 148, 150, and 156's Privy Seal classification, item 164's date and Essex locality, item 178's reference and place interpretation, item 234's date, item 238's print-versus-electronic heading conflict, item 240's date, and item 241's 1436 date and deed-poll form.

lineage.summary: ChancEng is a U-M XML selection of 241 documentary texts from Fisher, Richardson, and Fisher's 1984 anthology, transmitted through OTA records 1284 and 1676. It is not one medieval work or manuscript. The edition cites 231 TNA-held source documents/passages and ten passages in seven BL Cotton codices. This manifest preserves those item-level mappings, distinguishes exact current catalog matches from parent-level resolutions, records known editorial errors, caches the public OTA objects and two complete BL manuscript facsimiles, and keeps medieval source-content rights separate from modern edition, encoding, scan, and provider-copy terms.

catalogSummary.author: Multiple anonymous and named documentary creators
catalogSummary.date: Selected records currently corrected to 1384–1455 (anthology/XML headings report 1384–1462); anthology published 1984
catalogSummary.editor: John H. Fisher, Malcolm Richardson, and Jane L. Fisher
catalogSummary.region: England, especially institutions associated with London and Westminster, with wider British and continental connections
catalogSummary.title: An Anthology of Chancery English

catalogingSubject.label: An Anthology of Chancery English: selected documentary texts
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection of 241 documentary texts from printed pp. 84–303 of Fisher, Richardson, and Fisher's 1984 anthology. It is not one medieval authored work, one manuscript, all contents of any cited archival object, or the complete printed volume. The source documents survive across 231 TNA citations and ten passages in seven British Library Cotton codices, with some current catalog matches only at parent-piece level.

contentParts.label: Signet and related documents
notes: The sequence and count are well supported; the category label is inherited from the edition and should not be treated as verified provenance for every item. Benskin reclassifies items 106 and 107 as Privy Seal material.
contentParts.summary: Items 1–109: 105 documents presented as Henry V Signet material (1417–1422), followed by four later documents (1434–1455). The edition's departmental and chronological assignments are not uniformly reliable.

contentParts.label: Privy Seal papers
notes: The sequence and count are well supported; the category label is inherited from the edition and should not be treated as verified provenance for every item. Benskin identifies items 106, 107, 148, 150, and 156 as Privy Seal material on administrative evidence.
contentParts.summary: Items 110–160: documents dated 1408–1455 and grouped by the edition as Privy Seal papers; later critique shows that some boundaries between Signet and Privy Seal material require reassessment.

contentParts.label: Proceedings in Parliament and Chancery
notes: The sequence and count are well supported; the category label is inherited from the edition and should not be treated as verified provenance for every item.
contentParts.summary: Items 161–232: petitions and other parliamentary or Chancery proceedings dated 1388–1455, including many Ancient Petitions now cataloged in TNA series SC 8.

contentParts.label: Indentures
notes: The sequence and count are well supported; the category label is inherited from the edition and should not be treated as verified physical form or provenance for every item. Item 241, for example, is a deed poll although its text calls itself an indenture.
contentParts.summary: Items 233–241: indentures and related documentary records. The edition/XML headings span 1384–1462; documented corrections to items 240 and 241 yield a currently corrected span of 1384–1455.

assertion.notes: This is a confirmed report about the edition's headings, not the corrected historical range. Benskin's manuscript collation refutes the 1462 date assigned to item 241.

dateStatements.display: 1384–1462 as reported by the anthology and XML headings

assertion.notes: This aggregate range applies the currently documented item-level corrections, including item 234 to 1425, item 240 to 1455, and item 241 to 1436. It remains provisional until all 241 items are rechecked.

dateStatements.display: 1384–1455 after applying currently documented date corrections

extent.description: The XML has 241 flat DIV1 text items. Four conceptual groups are reconstructed from the edition sequence and a scholarly description; 475 P elements, 3,978 LB milestones, and printed page milestones for pp. 84–303 are encoding metrics rather than work counts.

formStatements.description: All 241 selected documentary texts are prose, though layout and documentary formulas vary.

scope.description: Edition-defined grouping; individual records may require reclassification.
Edition-defined grouping; individual records may require reclassification.
Edition-defined grouping; individual records may require reclassification.
Edition-defined grouping; individual records may require reclassification.
Embedded formulas and short passages; some accompanying Latin is mentioned but omitted by the edition.
Occasional embedded or accompanying Middle French or Anglo-French material in the documentary sources.
Modern English headings, source labels, and short editorial annotations occur across the selection.

notes: Edition-derived group names are discovery aids, not verified statements that every source was created by the named administrative office.
The XML's missing and out-of-order printed-page milestones are encoding anomalies; they are not evidence that the corresponding edition pages or manuscript leaves are absent.
Detailed item-level archival and facsimile access is maintained in the linked lineage manifest rather than flattened into this descriptive record.

next_steps: Compare each selected text with a current facsimile or repository reproduction.
Record corrections item by item without silently normalizing the inherited edition.
openQuestions.question: Which of the 241 transcriptions, dates, localities, source references, and departmental labels can be confirmed against the physical records?

next_steps: Seek written permission or clarification from U-M, OTA, and the relevant edition rightsholder before commercial or broad republication.
Keep provider image and archival-reproduction permissions separate from text rights.
openQuestions.question: What permissions govern redistribution and commercial/POD reuse when U-M labels its electronic text no-copyright but OTA and the CME source folder impose CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 and the 1984 edition remains copyrighted?

next_steps: Consult the printed edition's apparatus and TNA series-level finding aids.
Ask TNA staff about unresolved legacy reference syntax where necessary.
openQuestions.question: Can the 145 anthology references currently resolved only to a parent catalog piece be mapped to exact modern item-level records, and what do colon-separated or malformed legacy references mean?

next_steps: Ask the British Library to reconcile historical and current foliation.
Retain both reported locators in the lineage record.
openQuestions.question: Why does anthology item 14 identify Cotton MS Vespasian C XII, f. 155, while the current BL catalog describes the matching John Hull order at f. 154r and the IIIF canvas labels the matching image f. 155r?

assertion.notes: The selection belongs to English governmental and documentary culture; this does not claim every record was physically written in England.

scope.description: Numerous records concern or were associated with London, but the edition's locality labels are not reliable composition-place statements.
Numerous royal and administrative records name Westminster or institutions based there.

assertion.notes: This collective responsibility covers multiple named and anonymous historical actors and avoids asserting either a single author or universal anonymity.

subjects.label: History and standardization of written English

summaries.value: A modern edited selection of 241 late-fourteenth- and fifteenth-century English administrative, legal, and political documents, organized as Signet-related material, Privy Seal papers, proceedings in Parliament and Chancery, and indentures. The corpus is important for documentary practice and the history of written English, but its 1984 transcriptions, archival references, dates, localities, and departmental labels require item-level checking against current catalogs and surviving witnesses.

The electronic item encodes printed pp. 84–303 and omits the 1984 volume's front and back matter and printed editorial notes. It therefore supports browsing the selected texts but is not a digital facsimile or complete electronic edition of the book.

assertion.notes: This is the edition and electronic-item title, not an assertion of organic medieval unity.
